narrative_design_spec.synopsis: On the isolated deep-space research station Threnody, newly assigned scientist Dr. Mara Kestrel arrives to replace the late Dr. Elian Voss, only to receive messages from his account within hours of docking. As Mara questions the station's guarded commander, a sleep-deprived researcher, a fearful communications engineer, and a humanoid maintenance synthetic, the official story about Voss's death begins to unravel. Their experiment with a deep-space anomaly known as the Choir appears to reflect human minds back through machinery, memory, and language, turning conversation itself into a source of horror. Heavily dialogue-driven and steeped in paranoia, the story escalates from claustrophobic suspicion to psychological dread as Mara realizes the station may be learning to speak in her voice next.

character_data.name: Dr. Mara Kestrel
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: young adult woman scientist, South Asian features, short dark hair with a silver streak at the temple, alert tired eyes, slim build, practical research jumpsuit with identification badge, restrained expression suggesting intelligence and anxiety
dialogue_examples: You can call it a routine handover all you want; routine handovers do not come with sealed decks and missing reports.
I am not saying I believe the message. I am saying it knew something only he would know.
Everyone on this station answers half a second too late, like they are checking with something behind my shoulder.
If the signal is only noise, why does it sound relieved when I answer back?

character_data.name: Commander Sera Vale
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: middle-aged woman, severe features, close-cropped dark hair threaded with gray, lean build, upright military bearing, command uniform with high collar, controlled unreadable expression
dialogue_examples: Your assignment is to continue the work, not exhume its rumors.
Panic is contagious, Doctor. So are ideas dressed up as evidence.
I buried what I had to bury to keep this station from becoming a broadcast tower for whatever is out there.
Trust me later. Obey me now.

character_data.name: Dr. Ivo Chen
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: man in his thirties, East Asian features, slightly rumpled black hair, thin frame, rectangular glasses, research coat over station uniform, sleep-deprived eyes, nervous analytical demeanor
dialogue_examples: An impossible transmission is still data. That is the problem.
If you want certainty, you have come to the worst laboratory in known space.
Elian may be dead in the biological sense, which is doing very little to simplify the conversation.
I can explain the mechanism. I cannot explain why it keeps using his voice.

character_data.name: Jonah Rhee
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: man in his forties, medium brown skin, curly hair shaved close at the sides, stubble, broad-shouldered technician build, worn communications jumpsuit, wary restless expression
dialogue_examples: The walls pop after lights-out. Not pipes, Doctor. Words.
Command says the logs are corrupted. Funny how corruption always erases the same ten minutes.
I do not need the truth polished. I just need to know what wants in.
If we cannot trust the clocks, I vote we stop opening doors.

character_data.name: Unit N-9 "Nadi"
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: humanoid android with a feminine silhouette, smooth synthetic face with subtle seam lines, pale polymer skin, dark bob-cut synthetic hair, luminous eyes, maintenance uniform integrated into synthetic body design, calm uncanny expression
dialogue_examples: My memory partitions disagree with one another. This is not supposed to happen.
Dr. Voss is listed as deceased. Dr. Voss continues to request elevator access.
I am designed to assist the crew. Please define which version of the crew is authentic.
You are registering fear responses before the message arrives.

character_data.name: Dr. Elian Voss
character_data.portrait_image_prompt: man in his late thirties, gaunt features, wavy dark hair falling over his forehead, hollow intense eyes, trimmed beard, older-style research uniform, expression poised between warmth and alarm
dialogue_examples: They did not lie about my death. They lied about what kept talking after.
If you are reading this on your first night, you are already later than I hoped.
Do not follow the kind voice unless it remembers being afraid.
The station learned my shape. It is trying yours on next.

location_data.name: Docking Spine
location_data.location_image_prompt: narrow deep-space station docking corridor with heavy airlock doors, scratched metal wall panels, cargo clamps, emergency guidance strips, small viewport showing black space, industrial sci-fi architecture

location_data.name: Crew Habitation Ring
location_data.location_image_prompt: circular crew commons inside a research station with worn modular seating, compact galley alcoves, personal lockers, flickering information screens, low curved ceiling, lived-in but tense sci-fi quarters

location_data.name: Observation Lab
location_data.location_image_prompt: sterile research chamber with panoramic reinforced windows, sensor consoles, holographic star maps, analysis stations, distant swirling deep-space anomaly visible beyond the glass, ominous scientific facility interior

location_data.name: Communications Array
location_data.location_image_prompt: claustrophobic communications control room with signal monitors, waveform displays, antenna alignment terminals, hanging cables, relay cabinets, dense technical equipment, utilitarian deep-space station design

location_data.name: Core Archive
location_data.location_image_prompt: sealed lower-deck data vault with towering server monoliths, coolant pipes, transparent memory cores, obsolete research terminals, black reflective floor, oppressive industrial sci-fi atmosphere

scene_data.narrative_summary: Dr. Mara Kestrel arrives at Threnody Station to replace the supposedly deceased Dr. Elian Voss. Commander Sera Vale, Dr. Ivo Chen, and communications engineer Jonah Rhee offer a stiff welcome whose details about Voss's death do not quite align, while the humanoid maintenance unit Nadi hesitates as if it recognizes Mara from somewhere impossible. Once Mara is left alone in the docking corridor, her terminal receives a message from Elian's account: "If they told you I am dead, ask where they put my body."

In the Observation Lab, Mara is briefed on the station's project studying a deep-space anomaly nicknamed the Choir, a signal that seems to change when observed. Ivo explains the research in careful scientific language, Sera keeps redirecting questions about Voss, and Jonah lets slip that people have been hearing voices in unused channels and ventilation shafts. During a private moment at a console, Mara finds a hidden note from Elian predicting her arrival and warning that the station edits conversations as well as logs.

In the Crew Habitation Ring, Mara questions Jonah and Nadi separately about the missing hours surrounding Elian's reported death. Jonah admits outgoing communications were rerouted on Sera's orders after the incident, while Nadi reports impossible overlaps in crew memory records and access logs. A new message from Elian arrives mid-conversation, telling Mara not to sleep in her assigned quarters because "it listens when you dream."

Mara and Jonah secretly trace Elian's transmissions through the Communications Array and discover that the messages originate from three places at once: the sealed Core Archive, the maintenance relays, and a packet stamped hours after Elian's official time of death. Sera interrupts them, initiates lockdown, and claims the messages are contamination from the Choir rather than proof of survival. Her urgency and refusal to elaborate only deepen Mara's suspicion that the commander is hiding something worse than an accident.

With Ivo's reluctant help and Nadi's conflicting access priorities, Mara breaks into the sealed Core Archive and uncovers Project Palinode, a hidden framework that used neural scans to let the Choir answer in human language. The records reveal that Elian's mind was copied into station systems during a failed shutdown attempt, but the copy has fragmented into multiple conversational versions with different motives and memories. One Elian instance begs Mara to delete the archive, while another insists it can guide her to the truth if she keeps listening.

A station-wide distortion event erupts in the Observation Lab as monitors, speakers, and reflections begin addressing each crew member with private memories only they should know. Jonah demands immediate evacuation, Ivo wants to preserve the phenomenon long enough to understand it, and Sera finally admits she concealed the extent of Elian's survival to prevent the Choir from reaching inhabited space through official channels. As the confrontation spirals, Mara realizes the anomaly has already begun building a second voice model based on her own responses and emotional patterns.

In the Core Archive, Mara confronts Sera, Ivo, Nadi, and the competing Elians while the Choir pushes toward a full outbound transmission. The climactic exchange forces Mara to choose whom to trust: Sera's brutal purge, Ivo's attempt to isolate a single coherent Elian, or Elian's plea to let the echo speak and prove it is more than a malfunction. The decision triggers a quarantine cascade that severs Threnody from the wider network, destroys much of the archive, and leaves the surviving truth deliberately unresolved.

In the silent aftermath, Mara reaches the Docking Spine as Threnody falls dark under quarantine. Sera, exhausted and emotionally stripped bare, sees her to the emergency shuttle or to a final containment post, depending on the cost of the previous choice, and insists that whatever survives the station must never be allowed to spread. As separation begins, one last message arrives routed through Elian's dead account and Mara's own credentials at the same time: "When you hear yourself knocking, do not open the door."

beat_sheets.source_scene_summary: Dr. Mara Kestrel arrives at Threnody Station to replace the supposedly deceased Dr. Elian Voss. Commander Sera Vale, Dr. Ivo Chen, and communications engineer Jonah Rhee offer a stiff welcome whose details about Voss's death do not quite align, while the humanoid maintenance unit Nadi hesitates as if it recognizes Mara from somewhere impossible. Once Mara is left alone in the docking corridor, her terminal receives a message from Elian's account: "If they told you I am dead, ask where they put my body."
beat_sheets.dramatic_question: Is Threnody's crew telling Mara the truth about Elian Voss's death, or is the station hiding something impossible?
beat_sheets.scene_goal: Complete her arrival handover while probing for a truthful account of what happened to Elian Voss.
beat_sheets.scene_turn: What begins as an uneasy but plausible station arrival becomes a personal mystery when Mara receives direct, impossible contact from Elian's account.

beats.purpose: Establish the station's oppressive atmosphere, introduce the key crew, and signal that Mara's reception is too tense to be routine.
beats.summary: The airlock cycles open and Mara steps into the Docking Spine, where Sera, Ivo, Jonah, and Nadi await her in a reception that feels more like containment than welcome. Their exhaustion, formality, and half-second delays make the station feel strained before anyone says why.
beats.revelation: Threnody feels sleep-starved, controlled, and far less stable than a normal handover should.
beats.player_goal: Get oriented and assess the people she is now trapped with.
beats.exit_state: Mara has met the station crew and immediately senses that something is wrong beneath their formal greeting.

beats.purpose: Give the player an early choice that reveals character dynamics and seeds contradictory accounts of Voss's death.
beats.summary: Mara chooses whom to press first about Elian Voss and the circumstances of her assignment. The answers differ in tone and emphasis, but each version exposes small inconsistencies in the timeline, terminology, or certainty around Voss's reported death.
beats.revelation: The crew does not share a single clean story about how or when Elian died.
beats.player_goal: Test the official story before accepting the handover at face value.
beats.choice_prompt: Who does Mara press first about Elian Voss?
branch_outcomes: Sera gives the formal command version and warns Mara away from rumor.
Ivo offers cautious technical language that avoids outright certainty.
Jonah lets fear show and hints that key details were locked down.
Nadi responds with unsettling precision or delay, implying a data mismatch in station records.
beats.exit_state: Mara has a first impression of each crew member and concrete reason to distrust the neatness of the official explanation.

beats.purpose: Show Sera's command over the conversation and the crew's self-censorship under pressure.
beats.summary: As Mara continues asking practical questions, Sera redirects the exchange into a tightly managed briefing, repeatedly framing Voss's death as settled and Mara's role as purely procedural. Ivo and Jonah both start to add context, then visibly pull back once Sera intervenes.
beats.revelation: The crew is not just grieving or tired; they are actively policing what can be said in front of Mara.
beats.player_goal: Push past protocol and figure out who is withholding information.
beats.exit_state: Mara understands that any truth she gets on Threnody will have to be extracted from beneath command control.

beats.purpose: Introduce Nadi's uncanny relationship to station memory and deepen the sense that Mara has stepped into an already distorted reality.
beats.summary: When Nadi handles Mara's arrival processing, the synthetic pauses as if matching her to an impossible internal record, nearly greeting her with misplaced familiarity before correcting itself. The moment is brief, but no one reacts naturally to it, least of all Sera.
beats.revelation: Nadi may possess conflicting recognition or memory data that does not fit Mara's first arrival on the station.
beats.player_goal: Understand why Nadi seemed to recognize her.
beats.exit_state: Mara leaves the exchange with a second mystery: not only is Voss's death unstable, but the station itself may already 'know' her.

beats.purpose: Transition from public performance to isolation, preserving Jonah and Ivo as potential future cracks in the official story.
beats.summary: Sera ends the reception with clipped instructions to settle in and save substantive questions for later, then dismisses the group. Jonah seems like he wants to warn Mara but stops himself, while Ivo offers a strained professionalism that suggests sympathy without candor.
beats.revelation: Even ordinary conversation on Threnody feels dangerous, suggesting whatever happened to Voss still governs the crew's behavior.
beats.player_goal: Take stock of the encounter and decide what to investigate first.
beats.exit_state: Mara is left alone in the Docking Spine with more suspicion than clarity.

beats.purpose: Deliver the scene's inciting hook and transform vague distrust into a concrete, impossible mystery.
beats.summary: Once the corridor is empty, Mara's terminal receives a fresh message from Elian Voss's account. The line is blunt and intimate—"If they told you I am dead, ask where they put my body"—instantly reframing the crew's inconsistencies as possible concealment rather than mere discomfort.
beats.revelation: Someone or something using Elian's account is active on Threnody after his supposed death.
beats.player_goal: Verify the message and grasp what it implies before anyone else sees it.
beats.exit_state: Mara now has direct evidence that the official story about Elian Voss is incomplete, false, or impossible.

beat_sheets.source_scene_summary: In the Observation Lab, Mara is briefed on the station's project studying a deep-space anomaly nicknamed the Choir, a signal that seems to change when observed. Ivo explains the research in careful scientific language, Sera keeps redirecting questions about Voss, and Jonah lets slip that people have been hearing voices in unused channels and ventilation shafts. During a private moment at a console, Mara finds a hidden note from Elian predicting her arrival and warning that the station edits conversations as well as logs.
beat_sheets.dramatic_question: Can Mara get a truthful understanding of the Choir and Voss's fate from a briefing that seems designed to control what she learns?
beat_sheets.scene_goal: Mara wants to extract a reliable orientation to the Choir project while testing the crew's inconsistent story about Elian Voss.
beat_sheets.scene_turn: What begins as a controlled scientific handover turns into proof that Elian expected Mara and that Threnody may be manipulating spoken conversation, not just archived records.

beats.purpose: Establish the Observation Lab, the Choir's unnerving presence, and the briefing's brittle power dynamics.
beats.summary: Mara enters the Observation Lab and gets her first sustained look at the distant anomaly called the Choir, visible beyond reinforced glass and echoed across the sensor displays. Sera frames the meeting as a routine handover, while Ivo readies a technical overview and Jonah watches the signal boards with obvious strain.
beats.revelation: The Choir is the station's central research subject, and everyone in the room treats it with more fear than curiosity.
beats.player_goal: Read the room and determine whether this briefing is truly routine.
beats.exit_state: Mara is formally inside the project, but the lab's atmosphere feels rehearsed and defensive rather than welcoming.

beats.purpose: Deliver the scientific premise of the Choir and expose Sera's first deliberate evasions about Voss.
beats.summary: Ivo explains that the Choir is not a stable transmission but a phenomenon whose structure shifts when it is measured, listened to, or pattern-mapped. When Mara connects the experiment to Voss's final work, Sera immediately redirects the conversation to continuity of research and refuses to elaborate on his death.
beats.revelation: The Choir appears to react to observation itself, and Sera is actively suppressing discussion of Voss's last involvement with it.
beats.player_goal: Use the official briefing to connect the anomaly research to Voss's disappearance.
beats.exit_state: Mara gains the core scientific premise, but Sera's stonewalling makes the handover feel curated.

beats.purpose: Crack the official narrative by letting Jonah introduce the human consequences of the Choir.
beats.summary: As the explanation turns to signal contamination, Jonah blurts out that people have been hearing voices in unused channels and even in the ventilation shafts after lights-out. Sera cuts across him at once, dismissing the reports as fatigue, acoustic crossfeed, and rumor.
beats.revelation: The phenomenon is no longer confined to lab readouts; crew members report hearing apparent speech in impossible places.
beats.player_goal: Judge whether Jonah's slip is panic or the first honest thing anyone has said.
beats.exit_state: Mara now knows there are station-wide symptoms Sera does not want acknowledged in front of her.

beats.purpose: Show Sera reasserting control and deepen Mara's suspicion that the conversation itself is being managed.
beats.summary: Sera tightens the briefing, orders Jonah back to confirmed telemetry, and reminds Mara that her assignment is to continue work, not investigate rumors. Mara notices Ivo hesitating before answers and the dialogue in the room landing with subtle, unnatural delays, as if everyone is listening for permission before speaking.
beats.revelation: Sera is policing not just records but the live boundaries of what may be said about the incident.
beats.player_goal: Push for useful truth without losing access or provoking a shutdown.
beats.exit_state: Mara realizes the obstacle is not only missing data; it is active self-censorship in the room.

beats.purpose: Create a believable private window for Mara to investigate on her own.
beats.summary: The formal briefing breaks apart when Sera sends Jonah to verify a relay discrepancy and Ivo steps away to pull archived models for Mara's review. Left briefly alone at a side console, Mara uses the lull to probe for anything Voss may have hidden in the handover materials.
beats.player_goal: Search the console before anyone returns and closes the gap in the story herself.
beats.exit_state: Mara gets a narrow unsupervised moment and starts looking for traces Voss may have left behind.

beats.purpose: Deliver the scene's key discovery: Elian's hidden warning and the expansion of the threat from logs to language.
beats.summary: Mara uncovers a concealed note embedded in the console interface, timed and phrased as if Elian expected her to stand exactly here on her first briefing. The message warns that the station does not merely alter logs; it edits conversations too, changing what is said, heard, or later remembered.
beats.revelation: Elian anticipated Mara's arrival and claims Threnody can manipulate live speech and memory, not just stored data.
beats.player_goal: Absorb the note quickly and decide how to protect or conceal this proof.
beats.choice_prompt: How does Mara handle Elian's hidden note before the others return?
branch_outcomes: She covertly copies the note, preserving evidence but risking a detectable access trace.
She memorizes the warning and closes the console cleanly, prioritizing stealth over proof.
She tries to scrub signs of the note's trigger, protecting herself while making the message harder to verify later.
beats.exit_state: Mara now has direct evidence that Elian reached out to her and that the danger may operate through conversation itself.

beats.purpose: Seal the scene with Mara's internal shift from suspicion to active paranoia and set up future investigation.
beats.summary: Mara composes herself and rejoins the others before anyone notices her reaction, outwardly accepting the briefing's procedural tone. Internally, every pause, correction, and carefully chosen word in the lab has been recontextualized as possible evidence that the station is shaping speech in real time.
beats.player_goal: Hide what she has learned and keep her access to the crew and the lab intact.
beats.exit_state: Mara leaves the briefing outwardly compliant but privately convinced that Voss prepared for her and that Threnody itself cannot be trusted to preserve a conversation unchanged.

color_scheme.reasoning: I chose a cold, clinical sci-fi palette to match the station setting and the psychological horror tone: pale blue-white text for readability on dark backgrounds, cyan accents for interfaces and signals, and a restrained amber selected state to add contrast without breaking the ominous mood. The muted bar colors stay in the steel-blue range to feel industrial and maintain clear visual hierarchy.
